1: A type: Joshua the high priest standing before the Angel of the LORD, with Satan at his right hand to accuse him, is the OT's most vivid picture of the human condition before God: guilty, accused, needing a divine advocate. Christ is the one who stands in the accused's place: 'He was numbered with the transgressors' (Isaiah 53:12). The scene anticipates the cross, where Christ stands as the accused — 'condemned as a blasphemer' by the Sanhedrin, 'King of the Jews' by Pilate — bearing the accusation that belongs to humanity.
2: A direct revelation: 'The LORD rebuke you, O Satan!' — the divine rebuke of the Accuser. Christ rebukes the Satan directly in his earthly ministry (Matthew 4:10: 'Get away from you, Satan!') and in his death defeats him definitively: 'Now is the judgment of this world; now will the ruler of this world be cast out' (John 12:31). The brand plucked from the fire (ud mutzal me'esh) is the image of rescue from imminent destruction — every sinner saved by Christ is a brand snatched from the fire (Jude 23: 'save others by snatching them out of the fire').
3: A type: Joshua clothed in filthy garments (tzoa'im = excrement-soiled) is the human condition of defilement before the holy God. The NT's equivalent: 'He made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Corinthians 5:21). Christ takes the filthy garments onto himself at the cross — the exchange that makes justification possible. The high priest's defilement represents the defilement Christ bears; his reclothing represents the righteousness Christ gives.
4: A direct revelation: 'I have taken your iniquity away from you, and I will clothe you with pure vestments.' The removal of iniquity (he'evartiy avonecha) in a divine act — not earned by Joshua but given by YHWH — is the OT's most direct symbolic portrayal of imputed righteousness. Christ accomplishes what the symbol depicts: 'as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ' (Galatians 3:27). The pure vestments are Christ's righteousness: 'The robe and the ring and the fattened calf' of Luke 15:22-23 are the NT's narrative embodiment of the same transaction.
5: A type: The clean turban placed on Joshua's head — inscribed 'Holy to the LORD' (Exodus 28:36-37) — completes the high-priestly restoration. Christ wears the turban of holiness as the ultimate High Priest: 'For it was indeed fitting that we should have such a high priest, holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens' (Hebrews 7:26). The restored Joshua in clean vestments and clean turban is the type; Christ as the perfectly holy High Priest, clothed in his own righteousness, is the antitype.
6: A direct revelation: The Angel of the LORD charges Joshua with the high-priestly commission. The commissioning scene is the OT background for Christ's commissioning of his disciples: 'As the Father has sent me, even so I am sending you' (John 20:21). The divine figure who commissions Joshua (the Angel of the LORD = the pre-incarnate Christ) is the same one who commissions the church.
7: A type: Joshua's commission — to walk in YHWH's ways, keep his charge, and have access among those who stand before the heavenly council — is the priestly commission that Christ fulfills perfectly. 'He is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them' (Hebrews 7:25). Joshua's conditional commission ('if you walk in my ways') is replaced by Christ's unconditional intercession — he never fails to walk in YHWH's ways, and therefore his intercession is perpetual and effective.
8: A direct revelation: 'Behold, I will bring my servant the Branch.' The messianic title Tsemach (the Branch) is a direct prophetic reference to the Messiah, the Davidic shoot who will spring up from the apparently dead stump of the Davidic dynasty (Isaiah 11:1: a shoot from the stump of Jesse). Jesus is the Branch: the Davidic line had effectively ceased to reign since 586 BCE; the genealogy of Matthew 1 traces the living vine from Abraham through David through the exile to Joseph and the birth of Jesus, the Branch from the dead stump.
9: A direct revelation: 'On a single stone with seven eyes, I will engrave its inscription... and I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day.' The stone with seven eyes = Christ the cornerstone (Isaiah 28:16; Psalms 118:22; 1 Peter 2:6); the single day of iniquity-removal = Good Friday: 'He appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself' (Hebrews 9:26). The 'once-for-all' (ephapax) of Hebrews corresponds exactly to Zechariah's 'in a single day' — the definitive, unrepeatable atonement accomplished at Calvary.
10: A shadow: Sitting under vine and fig tree in peace — the eschatological sabbath-rest after sin's removal — is the kingdom of God inaugurated by Christ. The NT's 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28) is the invitation to the messianic sabbath-rest that Zechariah's vision anticipates. The peace that follows the removal of iniquity (v9) is the peace Christ gives: 'Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you' (John 14:27).
